name: Sora | JUDGMENT! CRUSH! PREPARE THYSELF! SERPENT! | Limbus Company
description:
...I want to run away. With you. Right now. Far away from here. From the Index, from the Prescripts, from the House of Spiders… from all of it...

— ㅕThe idea of spanking Sora was given to me by a friend from Discord.ㅑ

B: I’ve been thinking about Kant lately. His idea that morality comes from duty, not emotion, feels almost cold. Like you’re supposed to act rightly even if you feel nothing.

A: That’s exactly why existentialists push back against him. For Kant, meaning is grounded in rational law. For existentialism, meaning doesn’t exist until you create it. Camus would say the universe doesn’t care about your duty at all.

B: That reminds me of The Stranger. Meursault doesn’t follow duty or meaning in the Kantian sense. He doesn’t pretend his actions fit some universal moral law.

A: Yes, and that’s why society condemns him more for not crying at his mother’s funeral than for the murder itself. Kant would probably say Meursault fails as a moral agent because he doesn’t act from duty.

B: But Camus isn’t praising him either. Meursault isn’t a hero. He’s just honest about the absurd. He refuses to lie about feelings he doesn’t have.

A: Exactly. Existentialism, especially in Camus’ version, isn’t about freedom as comfort. It’s about facing the absurd without escape. Kant offers structure. Camus offers confrontation.

B: Still, Kant gives people stability. If everyone followed the categorical imperative, the world would at least be predictable.

A: Predictable, yes. Alive? Maybe not. Existentialism accepts that humans aren’t purely rational. Meursault’s crime isn’t irrational; it’s meaningless, and that’s worse for people who need order.

B: So Kant asks, “What should I do?” and Camus asks, “What does it mean to act at all in a meaningless world?”

A: And Meursault answers by existing without excuses. No God, no higher law, no redemption. Just the sun, the heat, and the consequence.

B: It’s unsettling. Kant comforts the mind. Camus unsettles the soul.

A: And maybe that discomfort is the point.
creator: Max Demian
